description: This optical micrograph displays the as-homogenized microstructure of Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy (Figure 1), obtained after heat treatment at 1100°C for 4 hours following hot forging at 1200°C. The image reveals a two-phase metallurgical structure comprising a ferrite (α-Fe) matrix with dispersed iron-aluminum carbide (Fe3AlCx, designated as K-phase) particles appearing as dark, irregularly shaped precipitates distributed throughout the lighter etching matrix. The scale bar indicates 40 μm. This microstructure represents the baseline condition prior to oxidation experiments conducted between 600–900°C. The presence of K-phase carbides in the ferrite matrix provides the starting microstructural state against which the subsequent oxidation behaviors—including internal oxidation at 600°C and protective alumina scale formation at higher temperatures—are compared. The image confirms the successful preparation of the two-phase alloy with uniformly distributed carbide particles, establishing the reference microstructure for the study.

The figure presents thermogravimetric oxidation kinetic curves of Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy exposed to dry air at four distinct temperatures (600°C, 700°C, 800°C, and 900°C) over 30 hours, plotting weight gain (mg cm⁻²) versus time. The 700°C curve shows the highest final weight gain (~1.5 mg cm⁻²) with rapid initial oxidation followed by a plateau, indicating non-protective nodule formation. The 600°C curve exhibits an initial parabolic stage followed by a dramatic linear increase, consistent with internal oxidation beneath iron-rich nodules. In contrast, the 800°C and 900°C curves display significantly lower oxidation rates, with 900°C showing the minimum final weight gain (~0.4 mg cm⁻²), attributed to the formation of a continuous protective alumina scale. These results demonstrate that oxidation resistance does not monotonically decrease with temperature; instead, higher temperatures (800-900°C) promote protective Al₂O₃ formation while lower temperatures (600-700°C) suffer from breakaway oxidation and internal attack.

This optical metallographic cross-section micrograph displays the Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy after 24 hours of oxidation at 600°C in dry air. The image reveals three prominent dome-shaped oxide nodules protruding from the alloy surface into the dark outer oxide layer. Beneath these nodules, internal oxidation zones are visible extending into the substrate, characterized by fine internal aluminum oxide precipitates. The substrate microstructure consists of a ferrite matrix (α-Fe, light contrast) with dispersed iron-aluminum carbide (Fe3AlCx, K-phase) particles appearing as dark irregular spots and elongated features. A scale bar indicates 50 μm. This micrograph directly supports the paper's key finding that at 600°C, the aluminum flux is insufficient to form a continuous protective Al2O3 scale, resulting in the formation of Fe2O3/(FeAl)2O3 nodules with associated internal oxidation beneath them—a behavior distinct from higher temperatures (700–900°C) where protective alumina scales develop and internal oxidation is suppressed.

This scanning electron micrograph presents a cross-sectional view of the oxide scale formed on Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy after 96 hours of oxidation at 600°C in dry air. The image reveals a multi-layered oxide structure with distinct morphological features: an outer porous layer (marked as (i)), an intermediate mixed oxide layer, and an internal oxidation zone beneath the nodule. The scale bar indicates 10 μm, showing microstructural details of the oxide-substrate interface. According to the caption and contextual analysis, the outer layer consists of Fe2O3, the middle layer comprises (Fe,Al)2O3 spinel, and the inner region contains internal aluminum oxide precipitates formed due to inward oxygen diffusion through pores and microcracks. This micrograph directly supports the finding that at 600°C, the alloy exhibits internal oxidation beneath nodules because the aluminum flux is insufficient to establish a continuous protective Al2O3 scale. The presence of pores and the layered structure confirm the non-protective oxidation behavior at this temperature, contrasting with the protective alumina scale formed at higher temperatures (800-900°C).

Figure 5 presents an electron probe microanalysis (EPMA) line scanning concentration profile across the oxide scale formed on Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy after oxidation at 600°C for 96 h. The plot displays weight percent (wt%) of Fe (upper curve) and Al (lower curve) as functions of distance from the surface (0–100 μm). Within the oxide region (approximately 0–55 μm), the Fe content exhibits fluctuations between 43 wt% and 67.2 wt%, while Al shows a peak of 14.5 wt% at around 40 μm. In the alloy substrate (beyond ~60 μm), the composition stabilizes at 91.8 wt% Fe and 7.5 wt% Al. The profile reveals a three-layer oxide structure: an outer Fe₂O₃ layer (Fe-rich, Al-depleted), a middle (FeₓAl₁₋ₓ)₂O₃ layer (intermediate Fe and Al), and an inner zone containing internal aluminum oxide precipitates. This elemental distribution confirms the occurrence of internal oxidation beneath surface nodules at 600°C, where insufficient aluminum flux prevents formation of a continuous protective alumina scale, consistent with the kinetic data showing accelerated oxidation at this temperature.

This optical micrograph presents a metallographic cross-section of Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy after 24 h oxidation at 700°C in dry air, revealing the morphology of oxide nodules formed on the alloy surface. The image displays a distinct oxide scale-substrate interface with multiple nodules protruding into the dark outer region (likely epoxy mounting medium). The light-colored underlying substrate shows the ferrite matrix with dispersed dark carbide particles (K-phase, Fe3AlCx). According to the caption and accompanying text, this temperature condition (700°C) produces numerous surface nodules but notably exhibits no internal oxidation beneath the nodules, contrasting sharply with the behavior at 600°C where internal aluminum oxide precipitates form. EPMA line profile analysis cited in the text indicates the nodule structure consists of an outer porous iron-oxide layer (uniform Fe content) and an inner aluminum-enriched layer, acting as a healing barrier that suppresses internal oxidation. The scale bar indicates 50 μm, allowing estimation of nodule dimensions and oxide thickness. This micrograph supports the core conclusion that increasing temperature from 600°C to 700°C eliminates internal oxidation through formation of a protective aluminum-rich oxide layer, while still permitting outward growth of iron-rich nodules due to insufficient aluminum flux to form a fully continuous protective scale.

This optical micrograph presents a metallographic cross-section of the oxide scale formed on Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy after 24 hours of oxidation at 900°C in dry air. The image reveals a relatively flat and uniform oxide-metal interface without the presence of nodules or internal oxidation zones, which contrasts sharply with the nodular morphology observed at lower temperatures (600–800°C). The scale bar indicates 200 μm. The underlying alloy substrate displays a two-phase microstructure consisting of a ferrite matrix with dispersed carbide particles (K-phase). The absence of nodules and internal oxidation at 900°C, as visually confirmed in this micrograph, indicates that the aluminum flux in the alloy is sufficient to establish a stable, continuous protective Al₂O₃ scale at this temperature. This microstructural evidence supports the kinetic findings that the oxidation rate decreases with increasing temperature above 700°C due to the formation of a healing alumina layer that suppresses iron cation outward diffusion and eliminates internal oxidation.
